summary: Car manufacturers optimize their vehicles for specific crash test benchmarks rather than overall safety, as revealed when IIHS added small overlap crash tests in 2012 and 2018. When these new tests were introduced, only Volvo scored well without modifications—nearly all other manufacturers had poor initial scores but quickly improved them once the tests became public benchmarks. This pattern mirrors benchmark gaming in other industries and suggests most carmakers prioritize cost savings over safety improvements that aren't measured by official tests.
key_points: When IIHS added driver-side small overlap tests in 2012, only Volvo scored well without modifications; most manufacturers had poor scores despite small overlap crashes causing ~25% of vehicle fatalities
After driver-side tests were added, manufacturers improved driver-side safety but neglected passenger-side until IIHS added passenger-side tests in 2018
Most crash test dummies are based on 1970s male proportions, leading manufacturers to optimize for male safety; systems like whiplash protection often work for men but not women
Real-world fatality data is noisy and difficult to interpret due to confounding factors, making crash test scores valuable despite their limitations
Tesla's safety reputation in tech circles contrasts with their mediocre test scores and defensive PR responses to criticism
money_quotes: Any time you have a benchmark that gets taken seriously, some people will start gaming the benchmark.

Despite the importance of small overlap crashes, from looking at the results when the IIHS added the driver-side and passenger-side small overlap tests in 2012 and 2018, it looks like almost all car manufacturers were optimizing for benchmark and not overall safety.

Although automotive companies can, in some cases, be held liable for unsafe designs, just optimizing for a small set of benchmarks, which must've resulted in extra deaths over optimizing for safety instead of benchmark scores, isn't something that engineers or corporations were, in general, held liable for.
Improving benchmark scores by cheating on benchmarks is much cheaper (and therefore higher ROI) than improving benchmark scores by actually improving the product.
We should have pretty strong priors that there was no 2014 model vehicle that was so safe that the probability of being killed in a car accident was zero.
key_insight: Manufacturers systematically optimize for known benchmarks rather than actual safety, revealing that without continuous introduction of new tests covering previously unmeasured scenarios, safety improvements stagnate in areas that don't affect public scores.
